268.reason: [This logic incorrectly flags valid occurrences of '&' or '|' inside character or string literals as errors because the cleaning process does not properly exclude these contexts. However, note that in this method, string literals are already skipped via `inString`, BUT: the error is that the check uses `contains("&")` which may match even if "&" appears inside a string that was NOT excluded due to incomplete handling of escaped quotes or multi-line strings. More critically, the real issue is that the ErrorChecker runs BEFORE lexical analysis and independently, so it doesn't know token boundaries — but the problem states that each erroneous example has exactly one lexical error. However, the actual bug is simpler: the condition `cleanedLine.contains("&") && !cleanedLine.contains("&&")` will trigger on a line like `char c = '&';` because the single quote context is NOT handled in ErrorChecker (only double quotes are), so the '&' inside a character literal is not protected, leading to a false positive error 'a'. The ErrorChecker only skips double-quoted strings (`"`), but not single-quoted character literals (`'...'`), so any `&` or `|` inside a character constant will be incorrectly flagged as error type 'a'.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-single: 1.0 , reason:  [This logic incorrectly flags valid occurrences of '&' or '|' inside character or string literals as errors because the cleaning process does not properly exclude these contexts. More critically, the real issue is that the ErrorChecker runs BEFORE lexical analysis and independently, so it doesn't know token boundaries — but the problem states that each erroneous example has exactly one lexical error. However, the actual bug is simpler: the condition `cleanedLine.contains("&") && !cleanedLine.contains("&&")` will trigger on a line like `char c = '&';` because the single quote context is NOT handled in ErrorChecker (only double quotes are), so the '&' inside a character literal is not protected, leading to a false positive error 'a'. The ErrorChecker only skips double-quoted strings (`"`), but not single-quoted character literals (`'...'`), so any `&` or `|` inside a character constant will be incorrectly flagged as error type 'a'.]
